title: RxJS and Observables in Angular
summary: Observables, operators (map, filter, switchMap, takeUntil), subscription management, and common Angular RxJS patterns.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between a Promise and an Observable?
choices: Promises are slower
Promises: eager (execute immediately), single value. Observables: lazy (execute on subscribe), multiple values over time, cancellable
Observables only work with HTTP
Promises can't handle errors
questions.explanation: A Promise starts executing when created; an Observable starts when subscribed. Promises emit one value then settle. Observables can emit many values, can represent streams (clicks, intervals), and can be cancelled by unsubscribing. Converting a Promise to Observable: `from(myPromise)`.

questions.prompt: Why is `switchMap` the right operator for a typeahead search but NOT for a form submit?
choices: switchMap is too slow for form submits

switchMap cancels the previous inner Observable when a new one arrives. For typeahead, cancelling stale requests is correct. For form submit, cancelling an in-progress save could leave the backend with partial state
switchMap doesn't work with HTTP calls
They are interchangeable — switchMap works fine for both
questions.explanation: switchMap is 'winner-takes-all' — only the latest inner Observable matters. For search: latest query wins, stale results discarded. For saves/deletes: you don't want to cancel a save because another action happened. Use concatMap (queue, in-order) or mergeMap (parallel) for write operations.

questions.prompt: What values does this emit?
questions.explanation: filter keeps only even numbers: 2, 4. map multiplies by 10: 20, 40. Operators run in sequence left-to-right in the pipe. filter runs first (drops 1, 3, 5), then map transforms the remaining 2 and 4.

questions.prompt: What memory leak does this code create?
choices: No leak — Angular automatically unsubscribes from all subscriptions
The interval keeps emitting after the component is destroyed — updating a destroyed component's state, preventing garbage collection
interval leaks only in production builds
Only leaks if the component is inside a *ngIf
questions.explanation: `interval` never completes. When the component is destroyed, the subscription continues — the callback still runs, trying to set `this.count` on a destroyed component. Fixes: async pipe (auto-unsubscribed), `takeUntil(this.destroy$)`, or storing the subscription and calling `this.sub.unsubscribe()` in ngOnDestroy.

questions.prompt: BehaviorSubject vs Subject — what makes BehaviorSubject useful for sharing current state?
choices: BehaviorSubject holds the last emitted value and immediately delivers it to new subscribers. Subject only delivers to subscribers present at emission time
BehaviorSubject is bidirectional
Subject doesn't support multiple subscribers
They are identical — just different naming conventions
questions.explanation: If you subscribe to a Subject AFTER the last value was emitted, you get nothing. With BehaviorSubject, you always get the most recent value on subscription. This makes it ideal for auth state, current user, settings — where late subscribers need the current value immediately.

questions.prompt: What does the async pipe do in Angular templates?
choices: Makes the template load asynchronously
Subscribes to an Observable/Promise, renders the emitted value, and automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed
Adds async/await to template expressions
Buffers template updates for performance
questions.explanation: The async pipe is Angular's safest subscription mechanism: it subscribes in ngOnInit, updates the view on each emission, and unsubscribes in ngOnDestroy. No manual cleanup needed. Always prefer `| async` over manual subscribe() in components.

questions.prompt: What does `catchError(err => of([]))` do in an HTTP request pipe?
choices: Stops the Observable after an error
On error, replaces the failed Observable with a new Observable emitting an empty array — the stream continues with a fallback value
Logs the error and re-throws it
Retries the request 3 times
questions.explanation: `catchError` intercepts an error and must return a new Observable to replace the failed one. `of([])` emits an empty array then completes — the subscriber gets `[]` as if the request succeeded with no data. Use this for graceful degradation. For retry: use `retry(3)` before catchError.

questions.prompt: You use `users$ | async` twice in the same template. What problem might this cause?
choices: Compilation error — async pipe can't be used twice
Two separate subscriptions are created — if users$ is an HTTP Observable, the HTTP request fires TWICE
The second pipe throws an error
No problem — they share the same subscription
questions.explanation: Each `| async` creates an independent subscription. For HTTP Observables (cold), each subscription triggers a new HTTP request. Fix: `*ngIf="users$ | async as users"` to subscribe once and reuse the `users` variable throughout the template. Or use `shareReplay(1)` on the Observable.

questions.prompt: Which patterns correctly prevent Observable memory leaks on component destroy?
choices: Using | async pipe in the template
takeUntil(this.destroy$) with a Subject completed in ngOnDestroy
takeUntilDestroyed(inject(DestroyRef)) — Angular 16+
Never subscribing — just calling .pipe() without subscribe
questions.explanation: async pipe, takeUntil with destroy$, and takeUntilDestroyed (Angular 16+) all correctly unsubscribe on component destroy. Just `.pipe()` without `.subscribe()` — nothing executes at all (Observables are lazy). Pick one strategy per component and use it consistently.

questions.prompt: forkJoin vs combineLatest — which should you use to make 3 parallel HTTP requests and act when all complete?
choices: combineLatest — waits for all to emit
forkJoin — emits once when ALL source Observables complete, with their final values. Perfect for parallel HTTP (each request completes once)
mergeMap — runs them in parallel
zip — they are equivalent
questions.explanation: forkJoin = Promise.all for Observables — waits for all to complete, emits an array of their last values. combineLatest emits every time ANY source emits (with latest from all) — not suitable for one-shot HTTP requests. Use forkJoin for parallel requests that each complete once.

questions.prompt: What does `debounceTime(300)` do in a typeahead search?
choices: Limits to 300 requests per second
Waits 300ms after the last emission before forwarding the value — if another value arrives within 300ms, the timer resets. Prevents firing a search on every keystroke
Batches 300 values together
Adds a 300ms delay to each value (not reset-based)
questions.explanation: `debounceTime` creates a 'settle period'. If you type 'a', 'b', 'c' rapidly, debounceTime waits until 300ms of silence, then emits 'abc'. Without it, each keystroke fires a search. Contrast with `delay(300)` which adds a fixed delay to every value regardless of subsequent emissions.

questions.prompt: Why is subscribing inside a service method generally an anti-pattern?
choices: Services can't create subscriptions
Services don't have an ngOnDestroy lifecycle — any subscription created in a service without proper cleanup leaks for the application's lifetime
Service subscriptions disable DI
It's not an anti-pattern — services should always subscribe
questions.explanation: Services live for the app lifetime (singleton). If a service subscribes to an interval or event stream, it must manually manage cleanup. The safest pattern: services return Observables, components subscribe (via async pipe). If a service must subscribe: use takeUntil with an app-level shutdown signal.

questions.prompt: What does `shareReplay(1)` add to an Observable?
choices: Retries the source 1 time on error
Multicasts the source to all subscribers and caches the last 1 emission — new subscribers get the cached value immediately without re-executing the source
Shares the Observable across modules
Replays the last 1 value only on complete
questions.explanation: `shareReplay(1)` is essential for HTTP calls used in multiple places. Without it: each subscriber triggers a new HTTP request. With it: first subscriber triggers HTTP, result cached; subsequent subscribers get the cached result. The '1' means remember the last 1 emission for late subscribers.

questions.prompt: `distinctUntilChanged()` in a search pipe prevents:
choices: Duplicate characters in input
Emitting a new search when the input value hasn't changed from the previous emission — avoids re-querying the same term
Concurrent HTTP requests
SQL injection in search terms
questions.explanation: If a user types 'react', pauses, then the cursor blinks without typing — `debounceTime` would emit again. `distinctUntilChanged()` drops the emission if the value is same as the last emitted value. Pair with `debounceTime` to get: settle → compare → search only if changed.

questions.prompt: Which are correct ways to create an Observable from existing data?
choices: new Observable(sub => sub.next([1,2,3]))
asObservable([1, 2, 3])
questions.explanation: `of(1,2,3)` emits each arg separately. `from([1,2,3])` iterates the array. `new Observable(subscriber => ...)` is the low-level constructor. `asObservable()` is a method on Subject to hide the emit capability — not a creation function with array argument.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between `tap` and `map` operators?
choices: They are identical
map transforms the value and forwards the new value. tap performs a side effect (logging, debugging) but forwards the ORIGINAL unchanged value
tap is asynchronous; map is synchronous
map is for arrays; tap is for objects
questions.explanation: `tap(x => console.log(x))` — logs x, passes x through unchanged. `map(x => x * 2)` — transforms x to x*2, forwards the new value. Use tap for debugging/logging without affecting the stream. Never use tap to transform values — use map.

questions.prompt: concatMap ensures what ordering guarantee that mergeMap does NOT?
choices: Response values are sorted
Inner Observables are subscribed sequentially — the next one only starts after the previous completes. Results arrive in the same order as inputs
concatMap is thread-safe; mergeMap is not
Only one request is ever in flight
questions.explanation: mergeMap: subscribe to all inners immediately (parallel). Results arrive in any order. concatMap: queue the inners. Only start the next when the current completes. Results arrive in input order. Use concatMap for: sequential file uploads, ordered database writes, undo/redo queues.

questions.prompt: What does `ReplaySubject(3)` do differently than `BehaviorSubject`?
choices: ReplaySubject holds only the latest value; BehaviorSubject holds 3
ReplaySubject replays the last 3 emissions to new subscribers; BehaviorSubject replays only the last 1. BehaviorSubject requires an initial value; ReplaySubject doesn't
They are identical with different buffer sizes
ReplaySubject works only with HTTP; BehaviorSubject for UI events
questions.explanation: `BehaviorSubject(null)` — replays 1 value to new subscribers, requires initial value. `ReplaySubject(3)` — replays last 3 emissions, no initial value needed. Use ReplaySubject for: chat history (last N messages), log streams (last N entries). BehaviorSubject for: current user, selected item (one current value).

questions.prompt: An Angular component template has `{{ user$ | async }}`. When does Angular unsubscribe?
choices: When the component's ngOnInit runs
When the Observable completes
When the component is destroyed (removed from DOM via *ngIf or navigation)
After 30 seconds by default
questions.explanation: The async pipe tracks the component's lifecycle. When Angular destroys the component (ngOnDestroy), the async pipe calls unsubscribe. This is automatic — you don't need to implement OnDestroy. If the Observable never completes (like a Subject), this prevents the leak.

questions.prompt: What anti-pattern does 'nested subscribe' represent?
choices: Using subscribe inside a for loop
Calling .subscribe() inside a .subscribe() callback — should be replaced with flatMap operators (switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap) to compose Observable chains without nesting
Having more than 5 operators in a pipe
Subscribing to the same Observable twice
questions.explanation: Nested subscribe makes code hard to read, impossible to cancel outer subscriptions, and prone to leaks. Instead: `outerObs$.pipe(switchMap(val => innerObs$(val))).subscribe(...)`. The flat-mapping operators compose chains, handle cancellation, and keep code linear.

flashcards.back: Observable: lazy (starts on subscribe), multiple values, cancellable, can be transformed with operators. Promise: eager, single value, not cancellable. Use Observables in Angular for streams, events, HTTP.
switchMap: cancels previous (typeahead). mergeMap: parallel, any order (analytics). concatMap: sequential, ordered (writes). Match to your use case — switchMap on writes is dangerous.
Auto-subscribes on init, re-renders on emit, auto-unsubscribes on destroy. Prevents memory leaks. Use `*ngIf="obs$ | async as val"` to subscribe once and reuse value.
Holds the current value — late subscribers get it immediately. Unlike Subject (must be present at emit time). Use for: current user, selected tab, any 'current state' that new subscribers need.
private destroy$ = new Subject(). .pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$)). ngOnDestroy: destroy$.next(); destroy$.complete(). Cleaner alternative: takeUntilDestroyed(inject(DestroyRef)) in Angular 16+.

Multicasts to all subscribers, caches last 1 emission. New subscribers get cached value without re-executing source. Essential for HTTP Observables used in multiple places — prevents duplicate requests.
Must return a new Observable. `catchError(err => of(fallback))` — graceful degradation. `catchError(err => throwError(() => err))` — re-throw. Place after retry() if using both.

forkJoin: emits once when ALL sources complete (Promise.all equivalent). combineLatest: emits when ANY source emits, with latest from all. forkJoin for parallel HTTP; combineLatest for live combined state.

project.brief: Design the complete RxJS pipeline for an autocomplete search component. Requirements: (1) User types in an input — each keystroke triggers a value. (2) Wait 300ms of silence before searching (debounceTime). (3) Don't search if the value hasn't changed (distinctUntilChanged). (4) Cancel any in-flight request when a new search starts (switchMap). (5) Handle errors gracefully — return empty array on fail (catchError). (6) Unsubscribe when the component is destroyed. (7) Display a loading indicator while the request is in-flight. Sketch the full pipe chain, explain why switchMap over mergeMap, and describe what happens to the loading state if the user types rapidly and cancels 5 requests.

checklist.text: Pipe chain: debounceTime(300) → distinctUntilChanged() → tap(setLoading) → switchMap(search) → catchError → tap(clearLoading)
switchMap rationale: latest query wins, stale responses discarded (no outdated results appearing)
Loading state: if 5 requests fired, 4 cancelled by switchMap — loading cleared after the 5th completes
Error handling: catchError returns of([]) — user sees empty results, not a broken UI
Cleanup: takeUntil(destroy$) or async pipe — subscription cleaned up on component destroy

hints: Use a Subject or fromEvent(input, 'input') as the source. Pipe the operators in order.
Loading: set loading=true in a tap before switchMap; set loading=false in a tap after (and in catchError).
switchMap cancels HTTP: Angular's HttpClient uses AbortController internally — switchMap triggers abort of the cancelled request.
Async pipe alternative: expose a results$ Observable from component and let template subscribe — no manual cleanup needed.
